comment_text.sn12.25:2.4: They believed in the efficacy of deeds, but not that such deeds created pleasure and pain. This seems odd, but it’s not irrational. Perhaps they believed that one’s deeds create a new life, but not the experiences of pleasure and pain in that life.
comment_text.sn12.25:3.1: In this discourse, the twelve links are merely hinted at here and in the mention of ignorance at the end.
comment_text.sn12.25:10.1: This forms the first part of AN 4.171, there addressed to “mendicants”.
comment_text.sn12.25:10.3: The tag avijjāpaccayāva (variant -ca), found also in the parallel at AN 4.171:1.3, appears tacked on to the completed sentence. Perhaps the purpose is to clarify that the mere presence of body, speech, and mind is not sufficient for kammic intentions, as arahants have body, speech, and mind but do not make kamma. The closing section of the discourse also addresses this topic.
comment_text.sn12.25:11.2: This anticipates the Abhidhamma term sasaṅkhārika, intentions that are prompted or instigated by another. For example, one might feel hungry and say, “Let’s eat”. Or someone else might say to you, “Let’s eat”. In both cases there is still moral responsibility for choices, but in the latter it is to some degree mitigated.
comment_text.sn12.25:11.3: “Deliberate” (sampajāna) means one is aware before, during, and after ( MN 61:12.1, Bu Pc 1:2.2.2). Compare the legal concept “pre-meditated”.
comment_text.sn12.25:14.2: The sentence has a conditional structure which limits and specifies its application. It is not that the body per se has disappeared. Rather, it is that body, namely the one dependent on ignorance, which no longer exists to create kamma. The sentence exemplifies specific conditionality: this body ceases, and that pleasure and pain no longer arise. An arahant experiences pleasure and pain as a result of past life choices, but does not actively create new kammic feelings.
comment_text.sn12.25:14.3: Adhikaraṇa as “foundation”  or “substratum” is attested in Sanskrit (eg. Tattvārtha Sūtra 1.7).

root_text.sn12.25:1.2: Atha kho āyasmā bhūmijo sāyanhasamayaṁ paṭisallānā vuṭṭhito yenāyasmā sāriputto tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā āyasmatā sāriputtena saddhiṁ sammodi.
root_text.sn12.25:1.3: Sammodanīyaṁ kathaṁ sāraṇīyaṁ vītisāretvā ekamantaṁ nisīdi. Ekamantaṁ nisinno kho āyasmā bhūmijo āyasmantaṁ sāriputtaṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn12.25:2.1: “Santāvuso sāriputta, eke samaṇabrāhmaṇā kammavādā sayaṅkataṁ sukhadukkhaṁ paññapenti.
root_text.sn12.25:2.2: Santi panāvuso sāriputta, eke samaṇabrāhmaṇā kammavādā paraṅkataṁ sukhadukkhaṁ paññapenti.
root_text.sn12.25:2.3: Santāvuso sāriputta, eke samaṇabrāhmaṇā kammavādā sayaṅkatañca paraṅkatañca sukhadukkhaṁ paññapenti.
root_text.sn12.25:2.4: Santi panāvuso sāriputta, eke samaṇabrāhmaṇā kammavādā asayaṅkāraṁ aparaṅkāraṁ adhiccasamuppannaṁ sukhadukkhaṁ paññapenti.
root_text.sn12.25:2.5: Idha no, āvuso sāriputta, bhagavā kiṁvādī kimakkhāyī,
root_text.sn12.25:2.6: kathaṁ byākaramānā ca mayaṁ vuttavādino ceva bhagavato assāma, na ca bhagavantaṁ abhūtena abbhācikkheyyāma, dhammassa cānudhammaṁ byākareyyāma, na ca koci sahadhammiko vādānupāto gārayhaṁ ṭhānaṁ āgaccheyyā”ti?
root_text.sn12.25:3.4: Iti vadaṁ vuttavādī ceva bhagavato assa, na ca bhagavantaṁ abhūtena abbhācikkheyya, dhammassa cānudhammaṁ byākareyya, na ca koci sahadhammiko vādānupāto gārayhaṁ ṭhānaṁ āgaccheyya.
root_text.sn12.25:4.1: Tatrāvuso, ye te samaṇabrāhmaṇā kammavādā sayaṅkataṁ sukhadukkhaṁ paññapenti, tadapi phassapaccayā.
root_text.sn12.25:4.4: yepi te samaṇabrāhmaṇā kammavādā asayaṅkāraṁ aparaṅkāraṁ adhiccasamuppannaṁ sukhadukkhaṁ paññapenti, tadapi phassapaccayā.
root_text.sn12.25:5.1: Tatrāvuso, ye te samaṇabrāhmaṇā kammavādā sayaṅkataṁ sukhadukkhaṁ paññapenti, te vata aññatra phassā paṭisaṁvedissantīti netaṁ ṭhānaṁ vijjati.
root_text.sn12.25:5.4: yepi te samaṇabrāhmaṇā kammavādā asayaṅkāraṁ aparaṅkāraṁ adhiccasamuppannaṁ sukhadukkhaṁ paññapenti, te vata aññatra phassā paṭisaṁvedissantīti netaṁ ṭhānaṁ vijjatī”ti.
root_text.sn12.25:6.1: Assosi kho āyasmā ānando āyasmato sāriputtassa āyasmatā bhūmijena saddhiṁ imaṁ kathāsallāpaṁ.
root_text.sn12.25:6.2: Atha kho āyasmā ānando yena bhagavā tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā bhagavantaṁ abhivādetvā ekamantaṁ nisīdi.
root_text.sn12.25:6.3: Ekamantaṁ nisinno kho āyasmā ānando yāvatako āyasmato sāriputtassa āyasmatā bhūmijena saddhiṁ ahosi kathāsallāpo taṁ sabbaṁ bhagavato ārocesi.
root_text.sn12.25:7.1: “Sādhu sādhu, ānanda, yathā taṁ sāriputto sammā byākaramāno byākareyya.
root_text.sn12.25:7.2: Paṭiccasamuppannaṁ kho, ānanda, sukhadukkhaṁ vuttaṁ mayā.
root_text.sn12.25:7.5: Iti vadaṁ vuttavādī ceva me assa, na ca maṁ abhūtena abbhācikkheyya, dhammassa cānudhammaṁ byākareyya, na ca koci sahadhammiko vādānupāto gārayhaṁ ṭhānaṁ āgaccheyya.
root_text.sn12.25:8.1: Tatrānanda, ye te samaṇabrāhmaṇā kammavādā sayaṅkataṁ sukhadukkhaṁ paññapenti tadapi phassapaccayā.
root_text.sn12.25:8.4: yepi te samaṇabrāhmaṇā kammavādā asayaṅkāraṁ aparaṅkāraṁ adhiccasamuppannaṁ sukhadukkhaṁ paññapenti tadapi phassapaccayā.
root_text.sn12.25:9.1: Tatrānanda, ye te samaṇabrāhmaṇā kammavādā sayaṅkataṁ sukhadukkhaṁ paññapenti, te vata aññatra phassā paṭisaṁvedissantīti netaṁ ṭhānaṁ vijjati.
root_text.sn12.25:9.4: yepi te samaṇabrāhmaṇā kammavādā asayaṅkāraṁ aparaṅkāraṁ adhiccasamuppannaṁ sukhadukkhaṁ paññapenti, te vata aññatra phassā paṭisaṁvedissantīti netaṁ ṭhānaṁ vijjati.
root_text.sn12.25:10.1: Kāye vā hānanda, sati kāyasañcetanāhetu uppajjati ajjhattaṁ sukhadukkhaṁ.
root_text.sn12.25:10.2: Vācāya vā hānanda, sati vacīsañcetanāhetu uppajjati ajjhattaṁ sukhadukkhaṁ.
root_text.sn12.25:10.3: Mane vā hānanda, sati manosañcetanāhetu uppajjati ajjhattaṁ sukhadukkhaṁ avijjāpaccayā ca.
root_text.sn12.25:11.1: Sāmaṁ vā taṁ, ānanda, kāyasaṅkhāraṁ abhisaṅkharoti, yaṁpaccayāssa taṁ uppajjati ajjhattaṁ sukhadukkhaṁ.
root_text.sn12.25:11.2: Pare vā taṁ, ānanda, kāyasaṅkhāraṁ abhisaṅkharonti, yaṁpaccayāssa taṁ uppajjati ajjhattaṁ sukhadukkhaṁ.
root_text.sn12.25:11.3: Sampajāno vā taṁ, ānanda, kāyasaṅkhāraṁ abhisaṅkharoti yaṁpaccayāssa taṁ uppajjati ajjhattaṁ sukhadukkhaṁ.
root_text.sn12.25:11.4: Asampajāno vā taṁ, ānanda, kāyasaṅkhāraṁ abhisaṅkharoti yaṁpaccayāssa taṁ uppajjati ajjhattaṁ sukhadukkhaṁ.
root_text.sn12.25:12.1: Sāmaṁ vā taṁ, ānanda, vacīsaṅkhāraṁ abhisaṅkharoti yaṁpaccayāssa taṁ uppajjati ajjhattaṁ sukhadukkhaṁ.
root_text.sn12.25:12.2: Pare vā taṁ, ānanda, vacīsaṅkhāraṁ abhisaṅkharonti yaṁpaccayāssa taṁ uppajjati ajjhattaṁ sukhadukkhaṁ.
root_text.sn12.25:12.4: asampajāno vā taṁ, ānanda, vacīsaṅkhāraṁ abhisaṅkharoti yaṁpaccayāssa taṁ uppajjati ajjhattaṁ sukhadukkhaṁ.
root_text.sn12.25:13.1: Sāmaṁ vā taṁ, ānanda, manosaṅkhāraṁ abhisaṅkharoti yaṁpaccayāssa taṁ uppajjati ajjhattaṁ sukhadukkhaṁ.
root_text.sn12.25:13.2: Pare vā taṁ, ānanda, manosaṅkhāraṁ abhisaṅkharonti yaṁpaccayāssa taṁ uppajjati ajjhattaṁ sukhadukkhaṁ.
root_text.sn12.25:13.4: asampajāno vā taṁ, ānanda, manosaṅkhāraṁ abhisaṅkharoti yaṁpaccayāssa taṁ uppajjati ajjhattaṁ sukhadukkhaṁ.
root_text.sn12.25:14.2: Avijjāya tveva, ānanda, asesavirāganirodhā so kāyo na hoti yaṁpaccayāssa taṁ uppajjati ajjhattaṁ sukhadukkhaṁ. Sā vācā na hoti yaṁpaccayāssa taṁ uppajjati ajjhattaṁ sukhadukkhaṁ. So mano na hoti yaṁpaccayāssa taṁ uppajjati ajjhattaṁ sukhadukkhaṁ.
root_text.sn12.25:14.3: Khettaṁ taṁ na hoti …pe… vatthu taṁ na hoti …pe… āyatanaṁ taṁ na hoti …pe… adhikaraṇaṁ taṁ na hoti yaṁpaccayāssa taṁ uppajjati ajjhattaṁ sukhadukkhan”ti.

translation_text.sn12.25:1.2: Then in the late afternoon, Venerable Bhūmija came out of retreat, went to Venerable Sāriputta, and exchanged greetings with him.
translation_text.sn12.25:1.3: When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he sat down to one side and said to him:
translation_text.sn12.25:2.1: “Reverend Sāriputta, there are ascetics and brahmins who teach the efficacy of deeds. Some of them declare that pleasure and pain are made by oneself.
translation_text.sn12.25:2.2: Some of them declare that pleasure and pain are made by another.
translation_text.sn12.25:2.3: Some of them declare that pleasure and pain are made by both oneself and another.
translation_text.sn12.25:2.4: Some of them declare that pleasure and pain arise anomalously, not made by oneself or another.
translation_text.sn12.25:2.5: What does the Buddha say about this? How does he explain it?
translation_text.sn12.25:2.6: How should we answer so as to repeat what the Buddha has said, and not misrepresent him with an untruth? How should we explain in line with his teaching, with no legitimate grounds for rebuttal and criticism?”
translation_text.sn12.25:3.1: “Reverend, the Buddha has said that pleasure and pain are dependently originated.
translation_text.sn12.25:3.4: In speaking like this, you would repeat what the Buddha has said, and not misrepresent him with an untruth. You would explain in line with his teaching, and there would be no legitimate grounds for rebuttal and criticism.
translation_text.sn12.25:4.1: Consider the ascetics and brahmins who teach the efficacy of deeds. In the case of those who declare that pleasure and pain are made by oneself, that’s dependent on contact. …
translation_text.sn12.25:4.4: In the case of those who declare that pleasure and pain arise anomalously, not made by oneself or another, that’s also dependent on contact.
translation_text.sn12.25:5.1: Consider the ascetics and brahmins who teach the efficacy of deeds. In the case of those who declare that pleasure and pain are made by oneself, it’s impossible that they will experience that without contact. …
translation_text.sn12.25:5.4: In the case of those who declare that pleasure and pain arise anomalously, not made by oneself or another, it’s impossible that they will experience that without contact.”
translation_text.sn12.25:6.1: Venerable Ānanda heard this discussion between Venerable Sāriputta and Venerable Bhūmija.
translation_text.sn12.25:6.2: Then Venerable Ānanda went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side,
translation_text.sn12.25:6.3: and informed the Buddha of all they had discussed.
translation_text.sn12.25:7.1: “Good, good, Ānanda! It’s just as Sāriputta has so rightly explained.
translation_text.sn12.25:7.2: I have said that pleasure and pain are dependently originated.
translation_text.sn12.25:7.5: In speaking like this, you would repeat what I have said, and not misrepresent me with an untruth. You would explain in line with my teaching, and there would be no legitimate grounds for rebuttal and criticism.
translation_text.sn12.25:8.1: Consider the ascetics and brahmins who teach the efficacy of deeds. In the case of those who declare that pleasure and pain are made by oneself, that’s dependent on contact. …
translation_text.sn12.25:8.4: In the case of those who declare that pleasure and pain arise anomalously, not made by oneself or another, that’s also dependent on contact.
translation_text.sn12.25:9.1: Consider the ascetics and brahmins who teach the efficacy of deeds. In the case of those who declare that pleasure and pain are made by oneself, it’s impossible that they will experience that without contact. …
translation_text.sn12.25:9.4: In the case of those who declare that pleasure and pain arise anomalously, not made by oneself or another, it’s impossible that they will experience that without contact.
translation_text.sn12.25:10.1: Ānanda, as long as there’s a body, the intention that gives rise to bodily action causes pleasure and pain to arise in oneself.
translation_text.sn12.25:10.2: As long as there’s a voice, the intention that gives rise to verbal action causes pleasure and pain to arise in oneself.
translation_text.sn12.25:10.3: As long as there’s a mind, the intention that gives rise to mental action causes pleasure and pain to arise in oneself. But this is only when dependent on ignorance.
translation_text.sn12.25:11.1: By oneself one instigates the choice that gives rise to bodily, verbal, and mental action, conditioned by which that pleasure and pain arise in oneself.
translation_text.sn12.25:11.2: Or else others instigate the choice …
translation_text.sn12.25:11.3: One instigates the choice after deliberation …
translation_text.sn12.25:11.4: Or else one instigates the choice without deliberation …
translation_text.sn12.25:14.1: Ignorance is included in all these things.
translation_text.sn12.25:14.2: But when ignorance fades away and ceases with nothing left over, that body, that voice, and that mind no longer exist dependent on which that pleasure and pain arise in oneself.
translation_text.sn12.25:14.3: That field does not exist, that ground does not exist, that basis does not exist, that foundation does not exist, dependent on which that pleasure and pain arise in oneself.”

variant_text.sn12.25:11.1: yaṁpaccayāssa taṁ → yaṁpaccayāyataṁ (sya-all, km); yaṁpaccayā yaṁ (mr)
variant_text.sn12.25:11.2: Pare vā taṁ → parevāssa taṁ (bj, pts1ed, pts2ed); pare vāyataṁ (sya-all, km)

reference_text.sn12.25:1.1: csp1ed12.274, csp2ed12.274, ms12S2_165, msdiv25, ndp13.34, pts-vp-pli2.38, sya16.45
